title: Lossless vs. Lossy Compression
concept: Compression shrinks file size by encoding data more efficiently; lossless compression can rebuild the exact original data, while lossy compression permanently discards some detail in exchange for a much smaller file.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what's the difference between a raster and a vector image?

options.text: Raster is a pixel grid; vector is math-based shape instructions
Raster and vector are two names for the exact same thing
Vector images always take up more storage than raster images

exercises.explain: Right — raster stores a fixed pixel grid, vector stores resizable shape formulas.

cards.title: Packing a suitcase two different ways
cards.body: Imagine packing for a trip. You could fold every item perfectly to save space without losing anything (nothing gets left behind). Or you could just leave half your clothes at home to make the suitcase lighter — it's smaller, but some things are simply gone. File compression works the same way.

cards.title: Lossless vs. lossy compression
cards.body: Compression shrinks a file's size by encoding its data more efficiently. Lossless compression can perfectly restore the original data with zero loss. Lossy compression permanently throws away some data to shrink the file much further, and that data can never be recovered.

cards.title: Shorthand notes vs. a summary
cards.body: Lossless compression is like writing notes in shorthand — every word can be perfectly expanded back to the original later. Lossy compression is like writing a short summary of a long book — smaller and faster to read, but the exact original sentences are gone for good.

cards.title: A zipped document vs. a compressed song
cards.body: Zipping a text document uses lossless compression — unzip it and every character is exactly as it was. An MP3 music file uses lossy compression — it strips out sound frequencies your ears barely notice, making the file much smaller, but the original uncompressed audio can't be perfectly rebuilt.

cards.title: "Lossy always means noticeably worse"
cards.body: It's tempting to think lossy compression always looks or sounds obviously broken. In reality, lossy formats are tuned to discard the details humans are least likely to notice, so a well-compressed lossy photo can look nearly identical to the original while being a fraction of the file size.

cards.title: Lossless vs. Lossy
cards.body: The core tradeoff between the two:

left.text:
Perfectly restores original
Larger file size
Good for text, code, master copies

right.text:
Some detail discarded forever
Much smaller file size
Good for photos, streaming media

exercises.prompt: What is the key difference between lossless and lossy compression?

options.text: Lossless can perfectly rebuild the original data; lossy permanently discards some detail
Lossless always produces a smaller file than lossy
Lossy compression is only usable for text files
There is no real difference, they are marketing terms

exercises.explain: Correct — lossless is fully reversible, lossy trades some permanent detail loss for a smaller file.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Once data is discarded by lossy compression, it can be perfectly recovered by decompressing the file again.
exercises.explain: False. Lossy compression's discarded detail is gone permanently — decompressing only approximates the original.

exercises.sentence: A ___ file format shrinks size mainly by discarding data the viewer is unlikely to notice.
exercises.explain: Lossy is correct — it targets the least noticeable detail to cut file size aggressively.

exercises.prompt: A hospital needs to archive original medical scans where every pixel must be recoverable exactly, with no alterations. Which compression approach should they use?

options.text: Lossless — no detail can be permanently discarded
Lossy — smaller files are always the priority
Neither — compression is never usable for scans

exercises.explain: Lossless is correct here — medical accuracy requires the exact original data to be recoverable, so no detail can be sacrificed.

exercises.prompt: A source-code repository needs perfect files with no corruption, while a video streaming app needs tiny files that load instantly on mobile data. Which compression type fits each, and why?

options.text: Lossless for the code repository (must be exact), lossy for video streaming (small size matters more than perfect fidelity)
Lossy for both, because lossy compression is always the modern default
Lossless for both, because code and video always need pixel-perfect accuracy
Lossy for the code repository and lossless for video streaming

exercises.explain: Exactly. Code must stay byte-for-byte exact, so lossless is required; streaming video prioritizes tiny, fast-loading files, so lossy's small quality tradeoff is worth it. Next: how one of the most common lossy formats — JPEG — actually squeezes down a photo.
